Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has 4 panels, each a 3x3 grid of colored squares. The bottom-right panel is inside a charcoal frame, suggesting it's a target/goal. Each panel has a center cell with a small pattern (gray/white/red pieces). The task likely involves clicking center pieces to change them or match patterns.
- Goal model: Match the panels to the target (bottom-right) configuration, or transform center piece patterns.
- Action model: MOUSE is the only action. Clicking on center pieces likely changes their pattern or triggers some transformation.
- Plan: Click on the charcoal frame body to test if it behaves differently.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 16 of about 43 target actions so far.
STALL: the board has not changed for your last 16 actions on this level — those actions had no visible effect. Stop repeating the same move; try a different action or target.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 16 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Looking at the unique hashes:
- id=33, color=g, pixels=20: top-left corner piece of bottom-right panel (unique hash)
- id=35, color=g, pixels=20: top-right corner piece of bottom-right panel (unique hash)
- id=62, color=g, pixels=20: bottom-left corner piece of bottom-right panel (unique hash)
- id=63, color=g, pixels=20: bottom-right corner piece of bottom-right panel (unique hash)
All 4 corner pieces have UNIQUE hashes! This means they are all different shapes (even though they might look similar). They're L-shaped corner pieces with different orientations.
Also notable:
- id=34, color=c, pixels=496: the charcoal frame body (unique hash)
- id=10, color=g, pixels=8: a gray 8-pixel object in the top-left panel's center piece (unique hash)
- id=23, color=W, pixels=8: a white 8-pixel object in the top-left panel's center piece (unique hash)
- id=47, color=W, pixels=12: a white 12-pixel object in the bottom-right panel's center piece (unique hash)
Wait, the 4 corner pieces have unique hashes, meaning they're all different shapes. This is interesting because the animation swaps their colors between gray and white. If they're all different shapes, then the animation is swapping the colors of 4 different-shaped objects.
Now, let me think about what the game could be. The 4 corner pieces are L-shaped and they're at the corners of the charcoal frame. They flash between gray and white. What if the game is about clicking on these 4 corner pieces to "remove" them or "transform" them?
But I've clicked on the corner pieces and the board hasn't changed. So that's not it.
Wait, let me look at the hashes more carefully. The 4 corner pieces have unique hashes, but they're all gray (color=g) and all have 20 pixels. If they have unique hashes, they must have different shapes. Let me look at their boundaries:
- id=33: [[32, 32], [32, 37], [33, 37]]... - top-left corner
- id=35: [[32, 56], [32, 61], [37, 61]]... - top-right corner
- id=62: [[56, 32], [56, 33], [59, 33]]... - bottom-left corner
- id=63: [[56, 60], [56, 61], [61, 61]]... - bottom-right corner
These are L-shaped corner pieces. The top-left one starts at [32, 32] and goes to [32, 37] and [33, 37]. The top-right one starts at [32, 56] and goes to [32, 61] and [37, 61]. Etc.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. I've been assuming that clicking on the board should change the board. But what if the game is designed such that clicking on the board DOESN'T change the board, and the goal is something else entirely?
For example, what if the game is a "puzzle" where the solution is to click on specific cells in a specific order, and the game only checks the sequence of clicks, not the board state? In this case, the animation is just feedback, and the board state is irrelevant.
But this seems unlikely for a grid-based puzzle game. Usually, the board state changes as you interact with it.
Let me try yet another approach. What if the game is about clicking on the cells in a specific order that corresponds to a "code" or "pattern"? For example, clicking on the cells that spell out a word or form a shape.
Actually, let me look at the problem from a different angle. The game has 4 panels, and the bottom-right panel is framed. The animation always affects the bottom-right panel's corners. What if the game is about "transferring" the patterns from the other 3 panels to the bottom-right panel?
For example, what if clicking on a cell in the top-left panel "copies" that cell's color to the corresponding cell in the bottom-right panel? But the board hasn't changed, so this doesn't seem to be the case.
OK, I'm going to try a completely different hypothesis. What if the game is about clicking on the cells in the bottom-right panel to change their color, and the changes are only visible after clicking on a "submit" or "confirm" button? The orange bar at the bottom might be the submit button.
But I've clicked on the orange bar and the board hasn't changed. So that's not it either.
Let me try one more thing: clicking on the charcoal frame body (id=34, pixels=496). The boundary is [[32, 38], [32, 55], [33, 55]]... This is the large charcoal rectangle that frames the bottom-right panel. Let me click on its center, which would be around row 46, col 46.
Wait, I already clicked on row=46, col=48 which is in the charcoal frame area. And that produced the same animation.
Hmm, let me try clicking on the exact center of the charcoal frame: row 46, col 46.
Actually, row 46, col 46 is in the center piece of the bottom-right panel, not the charcoal frame. The charcoal frame is at rows 32-61, cols 38-55 (approximately). The center of the frame would be around row 46, col 46... but that's inside the center piece.
Let me look at the ascii output more carefully. The charcoal frame is a large rectangle that surrounds the bottom-right panel. The 4 corner pieces are at the corners of this frame.
Actually, I just realized something. The 4 corner pieces have unique hashes, meaning they're all different shapes. But they're all L-shaped corner pieces. If they're different shapes, they might be rotated or mirrored versions of each other.
What if the game is about clicking on the corner pieces to rotate them? For example, clicking on the top-left corner piece rotates it 90 degrees clockwise. But the board hasn't changed, so this doesn't seem to be the case.
OK, I'm really stuck. Let me try one more radical approach: clicking on the black background (id=0, pixels=2160). The boundary is [[0, 0], [0, 63], [62, 63]]... This is the large black background that surrounds all 4 panels. Let me click on its center, which would be around row 31, col 31.
Wait, I already clicked on row=31, col=31 which is in the black background area. And that produced the same animation.
I'm going to try clicking on the very top-left corner of the board: row 0, col 0.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: 16 clicks, all identical animation, NO board changes. The 4 corner pieces of the bottom-right panel have unique hashes (different shapes). Let me try clicking on the very top-left corner of the board (row 0, col 0) which is part of the black background.

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the very top-left corner of the board
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 0, 'col': 0}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
